answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has not paid your holiday pay, you should first check your employment contract and any company policies to confirm your entitlement. Holiday pay is your right for the time you take off work as holiday, and your employer must pay you for this. If you believe you are owed holiday pay, try talking to your employer to explain the issue and show any evidence you have, such as your contract or payslips.

If talking to your employer does not resolve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance with them. If the issue remains unresolved, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unpaid holiday pay. It is important to keep records of your communications and any evidence of your entitlement. You can also contact ACAS for advice on holiday pay issues.

Step 1: Check your contract and company policies for holiday pay entitlement.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer and explain the issue with evidence.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if the problem is not resolved.  
Step 4: Consider legal action or contact ACAS for further advice.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you work irregular hours, your holiday pay is calculated based on the average pay you have received for the hours you have worked. This means your employer should look at your pay over the previous 52 weeks (or fewer if you have worked less time) and calculate an average weekly pay from that. Only the hours you actually worked and were paid for are counted in this calculation. Your holiday pay should reflect your "normal" rate of pay, which includes things like commission and regular overtime but usually excludes bonuses.

For irregular-hours workers, all statutory holiday leave must be paid at this normal rate of pay. You can use the holiday entitlement calculator on GOV.UK to help work out how much holiday pay you should receive. If your leave year began on or before 31 March 2024, different rules may apply, but generally, holiday pay is based on the average pay for hours worked.

Step 1: Calculate your average hourly or weekly pay over the last 52 weeks (or the weeks you have worked if less than 52).  
Step 2: Multiply this average by the number of hours you would normally work in a week to get your average weekly pay.  
Step 3: Use this average weekly pay to calculate the pay for your statutory holiday entitlement (5.6 weeks per year).  

If you have questions or need help calculating your holiday pay, you can contact the Acas helpline.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, an employer cannot include holiday pay in your hourly rate if you are a regular worker. This practice is known as "rolled-up holiday pay" and is generally not allowed. However, for workers with irregular hours or part-year workers, employers can use rolled-up holiday pay, but only if the leave year began on or before 31 March 2024. After this date, the rules have changed, and rolled-up holiday pay is no longer permitted for these workers either.

If you work irregular hours or part-year, your holiday pay should be calculated based on the hours you have worked rather than being included in your hourly rate. If you have questions about your specific situation, you can contact ACAS for guidance.

Step 1: Check if you are a regular worker or if you have irregular hours or part-year work.  
Step 2: Confirm when your leave year started (before or after 31 March 2024).  
Step 3: If you believe your employer is including holiday pay in your hourly rate unlawfully, contact ACAS or Citizens Advice for help.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, most workers are legally entitled to a minimum of 5.6 weeks' paid holiday per year, which is known as statutory annual leave. For someone working a standard 5-day week, this equates to 28 days of paid holiday annually. If you work part-time, your entitlement is proportional to the number of days or hours you work. For example, if you work 2 days a week, you would be entitled to 11.2 days of paid holiday (2 days × 5.6 weeks). This entitlement includes time accrued during periods such as maternity, paternity, adoption leave, or sick leave.

Employers may include bank holidays as part of your statutory leave, but they are not required to do so. Some employers offer enhanced or contractual holiday entitlement above the legal minimum, so you should check your employment contract for any additional holiday rights. If you are unsure about your exact entitlement, you can use the holiday entitlement calculator on the GOV.UK website or speak to your employer or HR department.

Step 1: Check your contract or written statement of employment particulars to see if you have any enhanced holiday entitlement.  
Step 2: Calculate your statutory holiday entitlement based on your working pattern (full-time or part-time).  
Step 3: Use the GOV.UK holiday entitlement calculator if you work irregular hours or part-year.  
Step 4: Contact your employer, HR, or the Acas helpline if you have questions or disputes about your holiday entitlement.

answer:

If your employer has not paid your wages on the agreed payday, you should first talk to your employer or payroll department to find out why the payment is late. It might be a mistake they can quickly fix. You should not have to wait until your next payday to receive the money owed. If talking to your employer does not resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance if your employer has a grievance procedure.

If the problem continues and your employer still does not pay you, you may be able to take legal action such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. Keep any evidence you have, such as payslips or your employment contract, to support your claim. Note that there is a strict deadline of 3 months minus one day from the date the wages were due to be paid to start tribunal proceedings.

The employee must leave because of the employer’s conduct, not for other reasons.

To claim constructive dismissal, the employee usually needs to have worked for the employer for at least two years and must resign promptly after the breach. It is important to follow a fair procedure and consider seeking advice before resigning, as this type of dismissal can be complex.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is illegal for an employer to discriminate against you because of your religion or belief. This protection covers all aspects of work, including hiring, pay, promotion, and dismissal. If you believe you have been treated unfairly at work due to your religion, you have the right to raise a complaint with your employer and expect them to take it seriously and investigate the matter.

Employers have a legal duty to prevent discrimination and harassment related to religion in the workplace. If informal resolution does not work, you can pursue a formal grievance procedure or seek advice from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice. You may also have the option to bring a claim to an employment tribunal if the issue is not resolved internally.

Step 1: Raise your concerns informally with your employer or HR department.  
Step 2: If unresolved, follow your employer’s formal grievance procedure.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for further support.  
Step 4: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal if necessary.

Harassment at work can be a form of discrimination if it is related to one of the protected characteristics under the Equality Act 2010, such as age, sex, race, disability, or religion. Harassment means unwanted behaviour linked to a protected characteristic that violates your dignity or creates a hostile environment. If your manager's behaviour fits this description, it could be considered harassment under the law.

If the harassment is not related to a protected characteristic but is still unwanted and makes your workplace uncomfortable, it may be classified as bullying rather than discrimination. Employers have a responsibility to prevent and address both harassment and bullying. You can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer, who should take your complaint seriously and investigate it.

Step 1: Document the incidents of harassment, noting dates, times, and details.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Workplace discrimination under the Equality Act 2010 means treating someone unfairly or less favourably because they have a protected characteristic. Protected characteristics include age, gender reassignment, being married or in a civil partnership, pregnancy or maternity, disability, race (including colour, nationality, ethnic or national origin), religion or belief, sex, and sexual orientation. Discrimination can happen directly (treating someone worse because of a protected characteristic) or indirectly (applying a rule that disadvantages people with a protected characteristic).

The law protects employees from discrimination at work, including during hiring, promotion, pay, training, and dismissal. It also covers harassment, victimisation, and bullying related to these protected characteristics. Employers have a legal duty to prevent discrimination and handle complaints seriously.

Step 1: If you believe you have been discriminated against, you can raise the issue informally with your employer or follow a formal grievance procedure.  
Step 2: If the issue is not resolved, you may consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.  
Step 3: You can also make a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limits set by law.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, workers are entitled to at least 5.6 weeks of paid holiday per year. Holiday pay is calculated based on a worker’s average weekly pay, which includes regular overtime, commission, and other payments related to length of service or qualifications, but usually excludes bonuses. For workers with regular hours, at least 4 weeks of holiday must be paid at their normal rate of pay, with the remaining 1.6 weeks paid at a basic rate. For irregular-hours workers or those working part of the year, all holiday pay should be at their normal rate.

To calculate holiday pay, employers look at the average pay over the last 52 weeks in which the worker was paid, or fewer weeks if the worker has not been employed that long. For monthly-paid workers, the average hourly rate is calculated from the last month’s pay and hours worked, then converted into a weekly figure.

Step 1: Determine the worker’s average weekly pay based on their pay pattern and hours worked.  
Step 2: Calculate the holiday entitlement as 5.6 weeks of this average pay.  
Step 3: Pay holiday at the appropriate rate depending on whether the worker has regular or irregular hours.

answer:

I can't act as your solicitor or give you legal advice. However, if your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe, you should first talk to them directly to understand why and ask them to pay you what you’re owed. Check your payslips and contract to confirm the amount due. If talking doesn’t work, see if your employer has a formal grievance procedure and raise a grievance about the unpaid wages. If you’re a member of a trade union, you could also ask them for help.

If these steps don’t resolve the issue, you may be able to take legal action by making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. You must do this within 3 months minus one day from the date the wages were due. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim in a small claims court instead. Collect all evidence such as payslips, contracts, and any written communication with your employer to support your claim.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe your employer owes you money, such as unpaid wages or unlawful deductions, you can take steps to resolve the issue. First, try to talk to your employer directly and explain the problem clearly, providing any evidence you have, such as your contract or pay slips. If this does not work, check if your employer has a formal grievance procedure and raise a grievance to formally complain about the issue.

If the problem remains unresolved, you may consider making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction from wages. You must start this process within 3 months minus one day from the date you should have been paid. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim unpaid wages through a small claims court instead. Keep in mind that taking legal action should be a last resort, especially if you plan to continue working for your employer.

Step 1: Gather all relevant evidence, including your employment contract, payslips, and any written agreements.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer about the issue and try to resolve it informally.  
Step 3: If informal talks fail, raise a formal grievance following your employer’s procedure.  
Step 4: If the grievance does not resolve the issue, consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limit.  
Step 5: If you miss the tribunal deadline, explore making a claim through the small claims court.  
Step 6: Seek advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser if you have one.

When writing an email to your employer about unpaid wages and mentioning a tribunal, it’s important to be clear, polite, and factual. ---

Subject: Unpaid Wages – Request for Payment

Dear [Employer’s Name],

I am writing to raise a concern regarding unpaid wages for the period of [specify dates or pay periods]. According to my records and contract, I am owed £[amount]. I have attached/pay slips and other evidence supporting this.

I would appreciate it if you could look into this matter and arrange payment by [reasonable deadline, e.g., 7 or 14 days from the date of this email]. If I do not receive payment by this date, I may have to consider making a claim to an employment tribunal to recover the money owed.

I hope we can resolve this matter promptly and amicably. Please let me know if you need any further information from me.

Kind regards,  
[Your Name]

---

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, workers are entitled to a minimum of 5.6 weeks’ paid holiday per year. Holiday pay is calculated based on the worker’s average weekly pay, which includes regular payments such as commission and overtime but usually excludes bonuses. For workers with regular hours, at least 4 weeks of holiday must be paid at their normal rate of pay, with the remaining 1.6 weeks paid at a basic rate. For irregular-hours workers, all holiday pay should be at their normal rate.

To calculate holiday pay, employers typically look at the average pay over the last 52 weeks in which the worker was paid. If the worker has been employed for less than 52 weeks, the average is based on the weeks they have worked. For monthly-paid workers, the average hourly pay is calculated from the monthly pay and hours worked, then converted into a weekly figure to determine holiday pay.

Step 1: Determine the worker’s average weekly pay using the last 52 weeks of pay data.  
Step 2: Identify whether the worker has regular or irregular hours to apply the correct holiday pay rate.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

When writing an email to your employer about unpaid wages and mentioning a possible employment tribunal claim, it is important to be clear, polite, and factual. You should explain the issue, state the amount owed, and give them a reasonable deadline to pay. You can also mention that if the issue is not resolved, you may consider taking legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. Avoid aggressive or threatening language, and keep the tone professional.

A simple example could be:

---
Subject: Unpaid Wages - Request for Payment

Dear [Employer's Name],

I am writing to raise a concern regarding my unpaid wages for the period of [specify period]. According to my records and contract, I am owed £[amount]. I have attached any relevant evidence for your reference.

Please could you arrange payment of the outstanding amount by [reasonable date, e.g., 14 days from the date of this email]? If there is any issue or discrepancy, I would appreciate your prompt clarification.

If I do not receive payment or a satisfactory response by this date, I may have to consider making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages.

I hope we can resolve this matter amicably.

Kind regards,  
[Your Name]  
[Your Contact Information]

---

Step 1: Gather evidence of the unpaid wages (e.g., payslips, contract).  
Step 2: Send a clear and polite email as above.  
Step 3: If no response or payment, consider raising a formal grievance or seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.  
Step 4: If unresolved, you may proceed with an employment tribunal claim within 3 months minus one day from the date the wages were due.

If you have issues with unpaid wages, such as unpaid holiday pay, sick pay, or statutory parental pay, you should first gather evidence like your contract or company policies. answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, workers are entitled to at least 5.6 weeks of paid holiday per year. Holiday pay is calculated based on a worker’s average weekly pay, which includes regular overtime, commission, and other payments related to length of service or qualifications, but usually excludes bonuses. For workers with regular hours, at least 4 weeks of statutory leave must be paid at their normal rate of pay, with the remaining 1.6 weeks paid at a basic rate. For irregular-hours workers or those working part of the year, all holiday pay should be at their normal rate.

To calculate holiday pay, employers typically look at the average pay over the last 52 weeks in which the worker was paid. If a worker is paid monthly, their average hourly pay is calculated for the last month and then multiplied by the number of hours worked in a week to find the weekly pay.

Step 1: Determine the worker’s average weekly pay based on their pay pattern and hours worked.  
Step 2: Calculate the total holiday entitlement (usually 5.6 weeks).  
Step 3: Apply the correct pay rate for the holiday period (normal or basic rate depending on the worker’s hours and contract).  
Step 4: Ensure holiday pay is paid when the worker takes their statutory leave.

Employers must have a valid reason for dismissal, such as misconduct, capability, redundancy, or a statutory restriction, and must follow a fair process. Settlement agreements can be used to end employment by mutual consent, but signing one usually means the employee cannot later claim unfair dismissal.

If you believe you have been unfairly dismissed, you should first raise the issue with your employer, possibly through a grievance procedure. If unresolved, you may consider making a claim to an employment tribunal, but it is advisable to seek advice from ACAS or a qualified solicitor beforehand.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, workers are entitled to at least 5.6 weeks of paid holiday per year. Holiday pay is calculated based on the worker’s average weekly pay, which includes regular overtime, commission, and other payments related to length of service or qualifications, but usually excludes bonuses. For workers with regular hours, at least 4 weeks of statutory holiday must be paid at their normal rate of pay, with the remaining 1.6 weeks paid at a basic rate. For irregular-hours workers or those working part of the year, all holiday pay should be at their normal rate.

To calculate holiday pay, employers typically look at the average pay over the last 52 weeks in which the worker was paid. If the worker has been employed for less than 52 weeks, the average is based on the weeks they have worked. For monthly-paid workers, the average hourly pay is calculated from the last month’s pay and hours worked, then converted into a weekly pay figure.

Step 1: Determine the worker’s average weekly pay using the last 52 weeks of pay or the weeks worked if less than 52.  
Step 2: Identify the worker’s normal rate of pay, including regular overtime and commission.  
Step 3: Calculate holiday pay by multiplying the average weekly pay by the number of holiday weeks taken.  
Step 4: Ensure at least 4 weeks of holiday are paid at the normal rate and the remaining 1.6 weeks at the basic rate for regular-hours workers.

If you believe your employer owes you money, such as unpaid wages or unlawful deductions, you should first try to resolve the issue informally by talking to your employer. Explain clearly what you think the problem is and provide any evidence you have, such as your employment contract or pay slips. If this does not work, check if your employer has a formal grievance procedure and consider raising a grievance to formally complain about the issue.

Keep in mind that taking legal action should be a last resort, especially if you plan to continue working for your employer.

Step 2: Talk to your employer informally to try and resolve the issue.
Step 3: If informal talks fail, raise a formal grievance if your employer has a procedure.
Step 4: If the grievance does not resolve the issue, consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the 3-month deadline.
Step 5: If you miss the tribunal deadline, consider making a claim in the small claims court.

If you want to write an email to your employer threatening to take your claim to an employment tribunal unless they pay you what you are owed, it is important to keep the tone professional and factual. You should clearly state the amount you believe you are owed, explain that you have tried to resolve the issue informally, and mention the deadline for making a tribunal claim (usually 3 months minus a day from when the payment was due). You can also say that you would prefer to resolve the matter without legal action but will proceed if necessary.

Here is an example of what you might write:

---

Subject: Outstanding Payment – Request for Immediate Resolution

Dear [Employer's Name],

I am writing to formally raise my concern regarding unpaid wages amounting to £[amount], which were due on [date]. I have previously raised this issue informally on [dates of previous communications], but the payment has not yet been made.

Please be aware that if this matter is not resolved promptly, I may have to consider making a claim to an employment tribunal. The deadline for submitting such a claim is 3 months minus one day from the date the payment was due.

I would prefer to resolve this matter amicably and without the need for legal action. I therefore request that you arrange payment of the outstanding amount by [reasonable deadline, e.g., 7 days from the date of the email].

Please confirm receipt of this email and your proposed course of action.

Yours sincerely,  
[Your Name]

---

Step 2: Send the email and keep a copy for your records.  
Step 3: If the employer does not respond or pay, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor before starting tribunal proceedings.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, employers must not discriminate against workers based on protected characteristics such as age, race, sex, disability, religion, or sexual orientation, as set out in the Equality Act 2010. Discrimination can be direct or indirect, and employers are required to take complaints seriously and take steps to prevent discrimination and bullying at work. If you experience or witness discrimination or bullying, you can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer.

Regarding pay, workers must receive payslips and be paid correctly, including at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage depending on age and apprenticeship status. Employers can only make deductions from pay in limited circumstances, such as for tax or National Insurance. If wages are unpaid or underpaid, workers can raise the issue with their employer or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Workers are entitled to 5.6 weeks of paid holiday per year. Holiday pay is calculated based on the worker’s average weekly pay, including regular overtime and commission but usually excluding bonuses. For workers with irregular hours, holiday pay is based on their normal rate of pay averaged over the previous 52 weeks.

Step 1: If you believe you are being discriminated against or underpaid, raise the issue with your employer informally first.  
Step 2: If unresolved, follow your employer’s formal grievance procedure or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.  
Step 3: Keep records of pay slips, hours worked, and any communications regarding your complaint.

You should explain the problem, state the amount you believe you are owed, and give them a reasonable deadline to resolve the issue before you take further action. You can mention that you have tried to resolve the matter informally and that if they do not pay you by the deadline, you may start an employment tribunal claim for unpaid wages or unlawful deductions.

Here is a simple example of what you might write:

---

Subject: Outstanding Payment – Request for Immediate Resolution

Dear [Employer's Name],

I am writing to raise a formal concern regarding unpaid wages for [specify period or amount]. Despite previous attempts to resolve this informally, I have not yet received the payment owed to me.

Please can you arrange for the outstanding amount of £[amount] to be paid by [reasonable deadline, e.g., 14 days from the date of this email]. If I do not receive payment by this date, I will consider making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages.

I hope we can resolve this matter promptly and avoid the need for further action.

Kind regards,  
[Your Name]

---

Step 2: Send the email and keep a copy for your records.  
Step 3: If the employer does not pay by the deadline, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about starting an employment tribunal claim.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, workers are entitled to at least 5.6 weeks of paid holiday per year. Holiday pay is calculated based on the worker’s average weekly pay, which includes regular payments such as commission and overtime but usually excludes bonuses. For workers with regular hours, at least 4 weeks of holiday must be paid at their normal rate of pay, while the remaining 1.6 weeks can be paid at a basic rate. For irregular-hours workers, all holiday pay should be at their normal rate. To calculate the average weekly pay, employers typically look at the last 52 weeks of pay, excluding any weeks with no pay by extending the period up to 104 weeks if necessary.

Step 1: Determine the worker’s average weekly pay by reviewing pay over the last 52 weeks (or up to 104 weeks if some weeks had no pay).  
Step 2: Identify the worker’s normal and basic pay rates, including regular overtime and commission.  
Step 3: Calculate holiday pay based on these rates, ensuring at least 5.6 weeks of paid leave is provided annually.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

When writing an email to your employer threatening to take your unpaid wages claim to an employment tribunal, it is important to be clear, professional, and factual. You should explain the issue, state the amount owed, mention any previous attempts to resolve the matter, and give a reasonable deadline for payment before you take further action. Avoid aggressive language and keep the tone polite but firm.

Here is a suggested structure for your email:

Subject: Outstanding Payment – Request for Immediate Resolution

Dear [Employer’s Name],

I am writing to formally raise my concern regarding unpaid wages amounting to [specify amount], which were due on [specify date]. Despite previous attempts to resolve this matter informally, I have not yet received the payment.

Please treat this as a formal request for payment. If the outstanding amount is not paid in full by [set a reasonable deadline, e.g., 7 or 14 days from the date of this email], I will have no option but to consider making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages.

I hope we can resolve this matter promptly and avoid the need for legal action.

Yours sincerely,  
[Your Name]

Step 1: Gather evidence such as payslips, contracts, and any previous correspondence about the unpaid wages.  
Step 2: Send the email and keep a copy for your records.  
Step 3: If the employer does not respond or pay by the deadline, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor before starting tribunal proceedings.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

I cannot provide legal advice, but I can share general information about employment disputes in the UK. If you have a dispute with your employer, such as unpaid wages or unfair treatment, you can first try to resolve the issue by talking directly to your employer or using their formal grievance procedure. If this does not work, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal, but there are strict time limits for doing so, usually within three months minus one day from the date of the issue.

For unpaid wages or unlawful deductions, you can also seek help from organisations like Acas or Citizens Advice, who offer free and confidential advice. They can guide you on your rights and the steps you might take. If you want to pursue a claim, it is often recommended to get advice from a qualified solicitor or an employment rights organisation.

Step 1: Try to resolve the issue informally by discussing it with your employer.  
Step 2: Use your employer’s grievance procedure if available.  
Step 3: Contact Acas or Citizens Advice for free advice and support.  
Step 4: Consider whether you want to make a claim to an employment tribunal before the deadline.  
Step 5: Seek legal advice if you are unsure about your situation or next steps.

Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe your employer has not paid you what they owe, such as unpaid wages, holiday pay, or statutory pay for maternity or parental leave, you should first check your employment contract and any relevant company policies to confirm your entitlements. For example, if you have not been paid for holiday days taken or mandatory training, your employer must still pay you at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for that time. If you think your employer has made unlawful deductions from your pay, you should gather evidence like payslips and contracts.

If you cannot resolve the issue directly with your employer, you can contact HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) for help, especially for disputes about statutory pay or minimum wage. It is important to act promptly, as there are time limits for making complaints, such as contacting HMRC within six months for statutory pay disputes.

Step 1: Check your contract and company policies to understand your pay rights.  
Step 2: Collect evidence of unpaid wages or deductions (e.g., payslips, contracts).  
Step 3: Contact HMRC or the Acas helpline for advice and to make a complaint if necessary.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, workers are entitled to at least 5.6 weeks of paid holiday per year. Holiday pay should be calculated based on a worker’s normal pay, which includes regular overtime, commission, and other payments related to length of service or qualifications, but usually excludes bonuses. For workers with irregular hours, holiday pay is calculated using an average of their pay over the previous 52 weeks (or more if some weeks had no pay). Employers must pay holiday pay at the worker’s normal rate for all leave taken.

If you want to check your holiday entitlement or how your holiday pay is calculated, you can calculate your average weekly pay based on your recent earnings and hours worked. If you believe you are not receiving the correct holiday pay, you can raise the issue with your employer or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Calculate your average weekly pay based on your last 52 weeks of pay and hours worked.  
Step 2: Check that your holiday pay matches this average rate for the holiday you take.  
Step 3: If you think your holiday pay is incorrect, raise the issue informally with your employer first.  
Step 4: If unresolved, consider formal steps or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

answer:

When writing an email to your employer about unpaid wages and indicating you may take the matter to an employment tribunal, it is important to be clear, polite, and factual. You should explain the issue, refer to your contract or agreed pay, and request payment by a specific deadline. You can also mention that if the issue is not resolved, you may consider making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages.

Here is a simple example you could adapt:

---

Subject: Unpaid Wages – Request for Payment

Dear [Employer's Name],

I am writing to raise a concern regarding my unpaid wages for [specify period or dates]. According to my contract, I should have been paid on [agreed pay date], but I have not yet received the payment.

Please could you confirm when I can expect to receive the outstanding amount of £[amount owed]? I would appreciate it if this could be resolved promptly.

If I do not receive payment by [reasonable deadline, e.g., 7 or 14 days from the date of this email], I may have to consider making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages.

I hope we can resolve this matter quickly and amicably.

Kind regards,  
[Your Name]

---

Step 1: Gather evidence such as your contract, payslips, and any communication about pay.  
Step 2: Send a clear and polite email like the example above.

For unpaid wages, you might also be able to take your case to a small claims court if the tribunal deadline has passed. It is often helpful to get advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser before taking formal steps.

Step 1: Contact Acas helpline for free advice on your situation.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, workers are entitled to at least 5.6 weeks of paid holiday per year. Holiday pay is calculated based on the worker’s average weekly pay, which includes regular overtime, commission, and other payments related to the job, but usually excludes bonuses. For workers with regular hours, at least 4 weeks of holiday must be paid at their normal rate of pay, with the remaining 1.6 weeks paid at a basic rate. For irregular-hours workers, all holiday pay should be at the normal rate.

To calculate holiday pay, employers look at the average pay over the last 52 weeks in which the worker was paid. If the worker has been employed for less than 52 weeks, the calculation is based on the weeks they have worked. For monthly-paid workers, the average hourly pay is calculated from the last month’s pay and hours worked, then converted into a weekly figure.

Step 1: Determine the worker’s average weekly pay based on their pay pattern and hours worked.  
Step 2: Calculate the holiday entitlement (usually 5.6 weeks).  
Step 3: Multiply the average weekly pay by the number of holiday weeks taken to find the holiday pay due.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).
If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe, you should first try to resolve the issue by talking to them and showing any evidence you have, such as your contract or written agreements. If this doesn’t work, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer. Most workers must be paid at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage, so if your pay falls below this, your employer may be breaking the law.

If you still don’t get paid, you might be able to take legal action through an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction from wages. It’s important to check your employment status (employee or worker) as this affects your rights. You can also get free advice from organisations like ACAS or Citizens Advice to help you understand your options and next steps.

Step 1: Talk to your employer and explain the problem with evidence.
Step 2: Raise a formal grievance if talking doesn’t resolve the issue.
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.
Step 4: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal if necessary.

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

When writing an email to your employer about unpaid wages and mentioning the possibility of an employment tribunal, it is important to be clear, polite, and factual. You should explain the issue, refer to any evidence you have (such as payslips or your contract), and state that you expect payment by a certain date. You can also mention that if the issue is not resolved, you may consider making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages.

Here is a simple example of what you might write:

---

Subject: Unpaid Wages – Request for Payment

Dear [Employer's Name],

I am writing to raise a concern regarding my unpaid wages for [specify period or date]. According to my contract and payslips, I am owed £[amount]. I have not yet received this payment, and I would appreciate it if you could look into this matter urgently.

If this is an oversight, please arrange for the payment to be made by [reasonable deadline, e.g., 7 days from the date of this email]. If I do not receive the payment or a satisfactory response by then, I may have to consider making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages.

I hope we can resolve this matter promptly.

Kind regards,  
[Your Name]

---

Step 1: Gather evidence such as your contract and payslips.  
Step 2: Send a clear and polite email as above.  
Step 3: If no response or payment is received, consider raising a formal grievance or seeking advice from a trade union or adviser.  
Step 4: If unresolved, you may proceed with a tribunal claim within 3 months minus one day from the date the payment was due.
